coredata.dc:title: Bletilla striata carbon dots with alleviating effect of DSS-induced ulcerative colitis
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Jiwen
Wang, Mengqing
Jiang, Xinjian
Liu, Yuting
Ge, Yunbo
Zhang, Chaoyan
coredata.dc:description: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a type of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) that significantly affected quality of life for patients. In this study, carbon dots based on Bletilla striata (BS-CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method and characterized by optical property analysis. In addition, the study measured the potential effect of BS-CDs on colonic histopathology and inflammation in dextran sulfate sodium (DSS)-induced ulcerative colitis. The results suggested that BS-CDs significantly increased colon length, improved colonic histopathology, and reduced the levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6) in colitis mice. Taken together, BS-CDs alleviate clinical inflammation by blocking pro-inflammatory cytokines which were expected to be a potential agent for the treatment of colitis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bletilla striata
Carbon dots
Ulcerative colitis
Inflammatory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assessment of colonic TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 levels
BS-CDs administration alleviated the symptom of DSS-induced ulcerative colitis
Effects of BS-CDs on pro-inflammatory cytokines

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of the BS-CDs. (A) Particle size and distribution of BS-CDs in H2O. (B) Absorption spectra of BS-CDs (red curve) and BS-CDs (black curve), respectively (Insets: the photographs of BS-CDs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light. (C) The emission spectrum of BS-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (D) The FTIR spectra of Bletilla striata (green curve) and BS-CDs (red curve). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Effects of BS-CDs on the symptoms of DSS-induced UC ICR mice. (A) Experimental treatment schedule. (B) DAI changes. (C) colon length. There are significant differences between groups at different levels, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. (D) H&E staining (scale bars 100 μm).

Effect of BS-CDs treatment on inflammation responses: (A) MPO; (B) TNF-α; (C) IL-1β; and (D) IL-6. There are significant differences between groups at different levels, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.
The synthetic route of Bletilla striata and experimental scheme for relieving ulcerative colitis induced by DSS.

head.ce:title: Bletilla striata carbon dots with alleviating effect of DSS-induced ulcerative colitis

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a type of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) that significantly affected quality of life for patients. In this study, carbon dots based on Bletilla striata (BS-CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method and characterized by optical property analysis. In addition, the study measured the potential effect of BS-CDs on colonic histopathology and inflammation in dextran sulfate sodium (DSS)-induced ulcerative colitis. The results suggested that BS-CDs significantly increased colon length, improved colonic histopathology, and reduced the levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6) in colitis mice. Taken together, BS-CDs alleviate clinical inflammation by blocking pro-inflammatory cytokines which were expected to be a potential agent for the treatment of colitis.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Carbon dots based on Bletilla striata

ce:section.ce:para: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) includes both conditions ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD) [1]. In contrast to CD, UC is a dynamic disease in which the degree of colorectal inﬂammation varies over time and manifests as mucosal inflammation extending into the proximal colon [2]. The primary clinical symptoms include bloody mucous stools, stomachache, and diarrhea, which seriously diminish the patients' quality of life. 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA) is a kind of primary drugs used in ulcerative colitis, but it has limited efficacy or associated with side effects [3]. In recent years, the therapeutic goal of IBD has shifted from simply controlling of symptoms to the so-called ‘deep remission’ [4]. Although anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive drugs have been used for treatment of UC in recent decades, they may have clinical limitations and side effects [5]. Therefore, this requires to find new potential therapeutic candidates.

Bletilla striata (BS), a well-known traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) with astringent, hemostatic, and swelling functions, was first recorded in the Shennong BenCao Jing, which commonly used to treat gastrointestinal bleeding [6]. Due to its hemostatic and myogenic functions Bletilla striata has been investigated for the treatment of UC. Clinical observation has suggested that it has a better effect on the improvement of bloody stool and the prevention of recurrence [7,8]. As a new type of nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted attention because of their excellent optical properties, tiny size (<10 nm), biocompatibility, and high electrical conductivity [9,10]. Therefore, CDs have been used in many fields, including bioimaging [11], biosensors [12], drug loading and drug delivery. In particular, bio-based CDs can exhibit the higher precursor activity [13].

In this study, carbon dots (CDs) prepared by the hydrothermal method with the precursor Bletilla striata are reported. The carbon dots based on Bletilla striata (BS-CDs) were tested for the physicochemical properties and the potential alleviating effect on DSS-induced ulcerative colitis (Scheme 1 ).

Bletilla striata was obtained from Guizhou (Guizhou, China). Dextran Sulfate Sodium Salt (DSS) with a molecular weight of 4,000 and 5-Aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA) were bought from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fecal occult blood gold gel strips were purchased from Zhuhai Baso Cell Science Technology Co., Ltd. (Zhuhai, Guangdong, China). Inflammatory cytokines of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 and myeloperoxidase assay kit were provided by Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute. (Najing, Jiangsu, China).

BS-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. Bletilla striata was mixed with deionized water in a ratio of 1:20 (1 g and 20 ml), then the mixture was heated in a drying cabinet at 180 °C for 6 h. The brown products were obtained after filtering through a 0.22 μM syringe filter for further use.

Size distribution was performed on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZEN 3600. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum was measured with a Mapada UV-1600 Spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured by a Nicolet iS5 instrument. The fluorescence spectrum was obtained by Japan spectrophotometer (F-7100).

The QY of BS-CDs was measured according previously reported methods [14]. Rhodamine B (RhB) was selected as a reference, whose QY at 340 nm is 0.31. The QY of BS-CDs calculated according to the following Eq (1):
(1)
Φ =
Φ R
×
A R
/
I R
× I / A × exp
( n )
/ exp
(
n R
)
where Φ is the QY, I is the integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index, and A is the optical density. The subscript R refers to the reference fluorophore.

The 36 male ICR mice (25–34 g, 8 weeks old) were purchased from Sipeifu (su zhou) Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. All mouse studies were approved by the Animal Research and Ethics Committee of Shanghai Ocean University (approval number: SHOU-DW-2023-074) and complied with international guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals. Okayasu et al. have first reported animal models of the chemical inducer induced colitis in mice [15]. An acute ulcerative colitis model was established to evaluate the efficacy BS-CDs in colitis. The mice were randomly divided into six groups (n = 6): control group, 3%DSS group, 5-ASA group, L-BS group (100 mg/mL/kg), L-BS-CDs group (100 mg/mL/kg), and H-BS-CDs group (300 mg/mL/kg). The procedure of the experiment was showed in Fig. 2A. The stool, weight, and other conditions were observed and recorded every day. On day 15, all mice were sacrificed by cervical dislocation after anesthesia, and then the colon length was measured and recorded. Finally, the colons were used for hematoxylin and eosin (HE) staining.

The DAI was determined according to Table 1 [16]. According to Table 1, the weight loss percentage score, stool trait score and fecal occult blood score of the mice were summed and averaged to obtain the DAI score of 3 for each mouse. Normal large stools are formed in shape, loose poop is a mushy, semi-formed stool that not adherent to the anus, while thin stools are watery and attached to the anus [17].

All collected colon tissues were washed in phosphate buffered solution to remove impurities and the distal colon (0.5 cm) was fixed in 10 % formalin solution. All colon tissues were embedded in paraffin after dehydration. The embedded tissues were cut into 5 μM sections using a microtome for H&E staining [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of colonic TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 levels
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse colon tissue samples were rinsed with cold 9 % saline to remove blood. The weight of the colon was then measured to prepare a 10 % tissue homogenate, which was centrifuged at 2000–3000 rpm at 4 °C for approximately 20 min followed by carefully collecting the supernatants to assess the levels of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 The assay was measured by the corresponding ELISA kits following the manufacturer's instructions.
Colon tissue was extracted according to a previous report [19], and MPO activity was measured using the appropriate ELISA kits following the manufacturer's instructions.

The characterization of BS-CDs was depicted (Fig. 1 ). The size distribution (Fig. 1 A) revealed that the size distribution of BS-CDs in water was 3.5 nm ± 0.6 and the polydispersity index (PDI) was 0.187 ± 0.03, following a normal distribution in the range of 1.0–10.0 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurements were used to determine the structure of the BS-CDs. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 1B confirmed that the functional moieties of the Bletilla striata and BS-CDs were almost identical. The absorption peaks at 3302 cm−1 in the spectrum belong to the O–H and N–H stretching vibration band [20]. Characteristic absorption peaks at 2902 cm−1 and 2868 cm−1 correspond to –CH2 stretching vibrations [21], the peak at the 1663 cm−1 to 1596 cm−1 assigned to CN and CC groups [22] and an obvious peak at 1020 cm−1 corresponding to the C–O–C bending vibration. Due to the presence of hydrophilic groups on the BS-CDs, the water solubility was good. The optical properties of BS-CDs were evaluated by detecting UV–vis absorption and FL emission at different excitation wavelengths. In Fig. 1C, Bletilla striata appeared peaks at 221 nm and 275 nm. The highest peak at 221 nm was attributed to the π→π* transition in the aromatic sp2 and the peak at about 275 nm was related to the n→π* transition for CO [23]. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of BS-CDs presented a highest absorption peak at 283 nm, which was assigned to the π→π* transition of CC, indicating the existence of a large π-electron system in BS-CD. The solution of BS-CDs was brown when viewed in daylight and turned pale blue when excited by 365 nm of UV (Fig. 1C Insets), which preliminarily indicated BS-CDs was photoluminescent. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of BS-CDs changes with the excitation wavelength, showing a trend of first increasing and then decreasing, illustrating that BS-CDs has an excitation-dependent emission spectrum which was the typical characteristic of fluorescent CDs. And the emission wavelength of BS-CDs was in the range of 400–500 nm, proving that it emits blue fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity of BS-CDs was the largest at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. 1D), and the QY of BS-CDs is 12.8 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BS-CDs administration alleviated the symptom of DSS-induced ulcerative colitis
ce:section.ce:para: The DSS-induced mouse model was established, and the animal experimental protocol is presented in Fig. 2 A. All mice were euthanized on day 15 and their colons were collected for further examination. The disease activity index (DAI) increased after intragastric administration with the highest DAI observed in the 3 % DSS group (Fig. 2B). In addition, colon length was measured to assess the effect of the different groups on the acute colitis. As shown in Fig. 2C, the colon length of the 3 % DSS decreased significantly, indicating a successful establishment of the colitis model. The groups treated with 5-ASA and the L-BS group showed normal colon length, whereas the L-BS-CDs and H-BS-CDs increased the colon length, suggesting that the BS-CDs inhibited the DSS-induced shorter colon length.

Furthermore, HE staining was used to analyze the structure and pathological changes of the colon tissues (Fig. 2D). The intact colonic mucosa, the abundant crypts, the enriched goblet cells in the control group and there was no inflammatory cell infiltration. In the 3 % DSS group, the crypt structure was disrupted and disordered, goblet cells were absent and the mucosal layer was thinner. After the oral administration of BS and BS-CDs, the colonic tissue destruction was alleviated to different degrees. In the L-BS-CDs group, the crypt's structure was intact and tightly arranged, with abundant goblet cells and no neutrophil infiltration.

To detect the effects of BS-CDs on neutrophil infiltration into colonic tissues, we measured the MPO activity in this study. As seen in Fig. 3 A, the MPO levels in colon tissues increased after administrating of 3 % DSS. These changes indicate oxidative damage and inflammation generated in mice following DSS induction. Importantly, the effect of the L-BS-CDs group on MPO levels exceeded that of the 5-ASA group. This indicates that the BS-CDs can reduce MPO levels in UC colon tissue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of BS-CDs on pro-inflammatory cytokines
ce:section.ce:para: As appeared in Fig. 3B-D, the levels of tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), the inflammatory cytokines interleukin-1β (IL-1β), and the inflammatory cytokines interleukin-6 (IL-6), in the colon tissues were all increased after DSS induction. Notably, the H-BS-CDs group displayed higher levels than the L-BS-CDs group, possibly due to an inflammatory reaction caused by excessive BS-CDs. These findings revealed that BS-CDs inhibited various inflammatory responses, the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines that are likely to be produced in the colon in colitis.

Ulcerative colitis (UC), an incurable condition categorized as inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), has been commonly treated with drugs aimed at alleviating the symptoms of UC [4,24]. Although the etiological factors of IBD are uncertain, the development of various animal models provides a research platform for studying IBD. The DSS-induced colitis model has been widely used because of its reproducibility and similarity to human ulcerative colitis [25]. Additionally, as a type of aminosalicylate drug, 5-ASA classed positive control drug can effectively relieve mild to moderate symptoms [26]. In particular, researchers have focused on the Chinese herbal medicine and its products that can ameliorate IBD [27], and several natural products have been evaluated in clinical trials for the treatment of UC [18,28,29]. Bletilla striata, a traditional Chinese medicine, contains various chemicals that support its pharmacological properties such as hemostasis and wound healing [30]. It is used for treating UC and can relieve the symptoms of UC by decreasing the secretion of the IL-6 and TNF-α [30]. Furthermore, the products derived from Bletilla striata have been shown anti-inflammatory activities in DSS-induced colitis. Zhu et al. studied that Bletilla striata oligosaccharides effectively inhibited the release of IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β [31]. In this study, carbon dots based on Bletilla striata (BS-CDs) significantly reduced colon length, preserved DAI and ameliorated DSS-induced histological changes. Myeloperoxidase (MPO), a lysosomal protein found in neutrophils is closely related to the disease status of IBD patients [32]. High levels of MPO activity were significantly reduced after treatment with BS-CDs, correspond to the results of H&E staining. Moreover, BS-CDs decreased the levels of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 to varying degrees had a similarity to the study of Zhu et al. The above results demonstrated the alleviating effect of BS-CDs on the colitis. Therefore, from the aspects of alleviating clinical symptoms and reducing inflammatory factors, BS-CDs may play a positive role in UC.

In summary, ultra-small CDs were synthesized from Bletilla striata (BS-CDs) using simply hydrothermal method. The basic characterization showed the size distribution, groups, and optical properties of BS-CDs. BS-CDs have high water solubility which making them suitable for nanomedicine. Meanwhile, the activities of Bletilla striata were retained and enhanced on BS-CDs. The vivo experiments indicated that the BS-CDs had better anti-inflammatory activities compared with Bletilla striata. The result demonstrated for the first time that BS-CDs can alleviate the clinical symptoms and pathological damage to the colon of UC, and its mechanism may be related to the decrease in the levels of MPO, TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6. These effects highlight the promising potential of BS-CDs in the treatment of IBD.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Jiwen Huang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mengqing Wang: Visualization, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xinjian Jiang: Methodology. Yuting Liu: Methodology. Yunbo Ge: Methodology. Chaoyan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
